findings.description: Nested <main> landmark elements - ensure the document has at most one main landmark at top level
findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 33. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: Section headings in PDF are underlined (Introduction, Background, Results, Summary) but in HTML they are rendered as larger bold headings without underline decoration.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'Introduction', 'Background', etc. centered and underlined. HTML shows them as left-aligned bold headings without underline.

findings.description: Table captions rendered as h3 headings instead of proper figure/table captions, creating a broken heading hierarchy. 'Table 1' and 'Table 2' and 'Diagram 1' are presented as h3 elements under h2 sections, but these are captions, not subsections.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses

Table 1. Longitude and Latitude...

,

Diagram 1. Biobase website...

, and

Table 2. Secchi Disk locations...

. In the PDF these are table/diagram captions, not section headings.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements - the HTML has both an outer <main> and an inner <main>, which is structurally incorrect but does not affect content fidelity.

Map 1 figcaption duplicates: The figcaption text 'Map 1. Area of lake showing treatment area.' appears as both a bold label in the PDF page and is correctly rendered, but the figure element has a nested <figure> inside another <figure> which is unusual but not duplicated content.
